body_txt: Villains frequently find themselves in conundrums that could easily be solved by finding the right person and putting a bullet in the offender's head . But for whatever reason, some villains continue to refrain from putting said bullet in said head. There may or may not be some Watsonian/in-story justification for this failure to take the direct approach. The villain could consider their opponent too worthy to dispatch swiftly . Or perhaps the villain has some kind of crippling personality flaw that says they have to do things the hard way. Maybe the villain is just straight-up nuts in a way that prevents them from even considering the direct approach . But whatever the Wastonian/in-universe reason might be, the Doylist/real-life explanation can always be boiled down to "because if he did just shoot him, the story would end right there and the bad guys would win ." Sub-Tropes include: Compare Third Act Stupidity , Contractual Genre Blindness , and Kill Him Already! (when the good guys are urging a quick kill). Someone might invoke this trope by using a Scheherezade Gambit . Contrast Combat Pragmatist and No-Nonsense Nemesis . Not to be confused with Just Eat Gilligan , although there can be overlap. Also contrast Once Is Not Enough when the hero KO's the villain and then chooses to flee instead of finishing him off. No other examples, please. If you, the viewer, are wondering why someone won't just shoot someone else, Headscratchers is the place to ask.
